phenomenon: Mars aeolian dunes and planetary sand cycles
key_measurement: Bagnold Dunes, Gale Crater: individual dunes up to ~30 m tall, crest-to-crest spacing ~100–300 m; global Mars sand flux 2.2 ± 1.0 m²/yr per meter width (Bridges et al., 2012, Geology)
institutions: European Space Agency (ESA) Mars Express Science Team
University of Arizona HiRISE Operations Center

sources.citation: Bridges, N.T., Ayoub, F., Avouac, J.-P., Leprince, S., Lucas, A., Mattson, S. (2012) 'Planet-wide sand motion on Mars', Geology 40(1):31–34

Ewing, R.C., Lapotre, M.G.A., Lewis, K.W., Day, M., Stein, N., Rubin, D.M., Sullivan, R., Banham, S., Lamb, M.P., Bridges, N.T., Gupta, S., Fischer, W.W. (2017) 'Sedimentary processes of the Bagnold Dunes: Implications for the eolian rock record of Mars', Journal of Geophysical Research: Planets 122(12):2452–2477
NASA/JPL-Caltech/University of Arizona — HiRISE (High Resolution Imaging Science Experiment) Image Catalog, Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter

visualization_compliance: Mars atmospheric pressure (~600–700 Pa, ~0.6% of Earth sea level) must inform saltation threshold — threshold wind speed ~10× higher than Earth equivalent
Dune morphology (barchan, transverse, linear) must be based on HiRISE or CTX image data — no Earth-analog substitution without labeling
Surface color must reflect Mars spectroscopy: basaltic grey-black for sand, reddish iron-oxide for dust mantle — not Earth brown/tan tones
No liquid water or vegetation in any Mars surface frame
Atmospheric haze/dust suspension must reflect Mars sky color (butterscotch/pink) not Earth blue
Label ILLUSTRATIVE on any artist reconstructions; cite HiRISE image ID for morphology reference frames
Scale bar in meters/kilometers required; north arrow with MRO orbit context on plan-view maps
